data.title: Wall Street is mixed amid Tesla and Alphabet earnings and rising yields
data.description:
Wall Street appears to show a cautious tone on Thursday, with the major averages trading mixed as investors weigh a wave of corporate earnings—highlighted by results from Alphabet and Tesla—against rising Treasury yields following an unexpected fall in jobless claims.

The blue-chip Dow (DJI) has shown the weakest signs as the index is lower by 0.3%. At the same time the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) finds itself up by 0.2% and the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) is also higher by 0.2%.

Yields have pushed up with the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rising by 3 basis points to 4.41%, and the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) has moved higher by 4 basis points to 3.92%.

The move up in yields comes as the latest jobless claims data fell, signaling continued resilience in the labor market—potentially giving the Federal Reserve more justification to maintain its current interest rate stance. 

Initial jobless claims for the week ended July 19 declined by 4K to 217K from 221K in the prior week (unrevised), and lower than the 227K consensus, according to data released by the Department of Labor. 

Amid heightened tensions between President Trump and the Federal Reserve, the president is slated to make a rare visit to the central bank on Thursday—marking the first such trip by a sitting U.S. president in nearly 20 years. The meeting is scheduled for 4 p.m. ET.

Also on the economic front, new home sales rose less than expected in the month of June. 

"The VIX remains tame and steady at moderate levels. This is an indication that investors are increasingly comfortable with the current trading environment, and expect it to continue. It also suggests a high level of complacency. Nevertheless, investors appear remarkably relaxed, and happy to add to their risk exposure. There’s plenty of optimism over tariffs, and a belief that trade deals will be agreed before next week’s deadline," David Morrison of Trade Nation said.

Earnings continues to heat up as well with Tesla (TSLA) down 8.8% as CEO Elon Musk said the company could face a "few rough quarters." The firm avoided a second consecutive top- and bottom-line miss, with profits in line with expectations. Also, Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG) was up 1.2% as the firm beat the expectations easily with another double-digit gain for revenues. 

“We would be buyers on any Tesla weakness. This is an AI story and the autonomous and robotics future is the future. Deliveries under near term pressure…but Musk is driving AI future and autonomous worth $1 trillion to the story. That’s our bull thesis and focus for Tesla,” long time tech bull Dan Ives said. 

“Alphabet’s quarter/comments should alleviate investor caution around the perceived risks of generative AI on Search biz. These concerns are overdone with Alphabet validating its ability to navigate this period of transition. Doubling down on AI Capex is bullish, $225 PT,” Ives added. 

Intel (INTC) is scheduled to report its latest quarterly results after the market closes on Thursday, with investors closely watching for updates on the company’s turnaround efforts and performance in key segments.

data.title: Block's Square rolls out merchant cash advances in U.K.
data.description:
Square, the payment network owned by Block (NYSE:XYZ), has launched merchant cash advances in the U.K., it said on Thursday, giving eligible businesses access to fast and easy access to funding.

Square Cash Advances provide an advance upfront to a business in exchange for a percentage of future card sales.

A fixed percentage of a business’s daily card sales will start being withheld two days after the funding date, and Square withholds a fixed percentage rate of future card sales until the balance is met.

The actual repayment time will depend on a business’s card processing with Square, meaning sellers pay back less when sales are slower and more when they’re strong. The percentage rate will be applied to the gross card sales, including tips and VAT paid by customers. This is also applied in addition to Square’s processing fees for transactions.

A recent Square survey found most small businesses agreed that cash advance would significantly help them with growing and running their operations.

data.title: Core Laboratories outlines Q3 2025 revenue guidance of $127.5M-$134.5M amid margin improvements and global expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Core Laboratories Inc. (CLB) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Lawrence V. Bruno highlighted that "Core continued to execute its strategic plan of technology investments targeted to both solve client problems and capitalize on Core's technical and geographic opportunities." He noted a 5% sequential revenue increase and improvements in operating income, margins, free cash flow, and EPS. He pointed to a 7% sequential revenue growth in Reservoir Description and a 3% increase in Production Enhancement, with margin improvements in both segments. Bruno emphasized continued demand for rock and fluid analysis and a rebound in laboratory assay services, despite ongoing geopolitical uncertainties.
  • Bruno reported, "Core Lab returned excess free cash to our shareholders by repurchasing more than 237,000 shares of company stock during the second quarter, a value of $2.7 million." He underlined further reduction in net debt by over $9 million, bringing the leverage ratio to 1.27, its lowest in eight years.
  • The company remains focused on introducing new offerings in key markets, keeping a lean organization, and returning free cash to shareholders while delevering. Bruno also discussed the successful deployment of Core's proprietary PVT technology and diagnostic services in Colombia and the expansion of geothermal tracer technologies in North America.
  • CFO Christopher Scott Hill stated, "Revenue was $130.2 million in the second quarter, up $6.6 million, or 5%, compared to the prior quarter and flat year-over-year." Hill explained the revenue drivers as a rebound in crude oil assay services and elevated levels of international product sales. He also noted, "Net income ex-items for the quarter was $8.8 million, an increase from $6.7 million in the prior quarter, but a decrease from $10.4 million in the second quarter last year. On a GAAP basis, we had net income of $10.6 million for the quarter."

Outlook

  • Senior Vice President Gwendolyn Y. Gresham provided guidance, stating, "Reservoir Description's third quarter revenue is projected to range from $84 million to $88 million, and operating income of $10.6 million to $12.4 million. Production Enhancement's third quarter revenue is estimated to range from $43.5 million to $46.5 million, with operating income of $2.9 million to $3.7 million." She projected total Q3 2025 revenue between $127.5 million and $134.5 million, with operating margins of 11% and EPS expected between $0.18 and $0.22.
  • Gresham cited volatility from tariffs, OPEC+ production changes, and macroeconomic factors as drivers of near-term uncertainty but maintained a constructive long-term outlook for international upstream activity.

Financial Results

  • Hill reported $130.2 million in revenue for Q2 2025, with $96.2 million in service revenue and $33.9 million in product sales. Service revenue grew 1% sequentially; product sales rose 19% sequentially, driven by bulk international orders and laboratory instrumentation sales.
  • Reservoir Description segment revenue was $86.3 million with operating income of $10.8 million, yielding 13% operating margins. Production Enhancement revenue was $43.9 million, with operating income of $3.1 million and 9% margins.
  • Cost of services ex-items was 77% of service revenue; cost of sales ex-items was 85% of revenue, improved from 91% last quarter. G&A ex-items for the quarter was $10.5 million. CapEx for Q2 was $3.5 million, with free cash flow at $10.4 million.
  • Net debt declined to $94.8 million, and the company renewed and expanded its credit agreement, extending maturity to July 2029.

Q&A

  • Sean W. Mitchell, Daniel Energy Partners, LLC, asked for color on a new proppant design partnership in West Texas. Bruno explained, "Our engagement was about them trying different designs of the proppant sort of particle size and the sorting...with the tracer flowback, we were able to confirm that the new design was producing better results from those intervals that deployed the revised design."
  • Mitchell inquired about new product or service offerings, especially in the Middle East. Bruno responded, "We've made quite an initiative into looking at ways of laboratory testing formation damage as people are introducing various different completion fluids into the well...we opened up our unconventional laboratory in Dammam, Saudi Arabia."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on technology developments, market expansion, and segment-specific growth, with no signs of skepticism or pressing concerns.
  • Management expressed confidence in both prepared and live remarks, emphasizing operational and technological positioning: "We have never been better operationally, or technologically positioned, to help our global client base optimize their reservoirs."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment shifted from cautious optimism amid sanctions and headwinds to a more confident outlook, supported by margin improvements and international expansion.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Sequential revenue grew 5% after a 4% decline in the previous quarter. Reservoir Description and Production Enhancement both saw margin improvements versus Q1, partially driven by a rebound in assay services and bulk international product sales.
  • Management tone strengthened, shifting from mitigating sanction impacts in Q1 to highlighting growth, deleveraging, and technological advancement in Q2.
  • Analyst questions in Q2 focused more on new offerings and expansion, compared to Q1's focus on margin progression and regional headwinds.

Risks and Concerns

  • Bruno and Gresham pointed to ongoing geopolitical conflicts, expanded sanctions, and volatility from tariffs as continuing risks impacting assay demand and commodity prices.
  • Management discussed steps to mitigate tariff impacts, highlighting that over 75% of service revenue is not subject to tariffs and that they are actively managing the effect of tariffs on imported raw materials.
  • Gresham noted, "Geopolitical conflicts, evolving trade and tariff dynamics, and volatile commodity prices continue to create uncertainty in the demand for laboratory services tied to the maritime transportation and trade of crude oil and derived products."

Final Takeaway

Core Laboratories emphasized its improved sequential financial performance, ongoing deleveraging, and strategic expansion in key international markets. Management reiterated its commitment to free cash flow generation, shareholder returns, and technological leadership while navigating ongoing geopolitical and macroeconomic challenges. The company projects steady activity in its core segments for the next quarter, underpinned by robust international demand and the introduction of innovative service offerings.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Flex outlines $6.5B data center revenue target for FY26 while expanding power capacity in Europe
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Flex Ltd. (FLEX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Revathi Advaithi highlighted an exceptional quarter, stating Flex delivered "positive results against our guidance" and emphasized ongoing profitable growth, particularly in the data center segment. She noted, "Our revenues were $6.6 billion, up 4%. Our adjusted operating margin was 6%, and we delivered adjusted EPS of $0.72, a record Q1 number for Flex."

  • Advaithi discussed the company's strategic shift, sharing that "our portfolio mix continues to shift as data center becomes a larger and more strategic contributor," with an expectation for the data center business to deliver approximately $6.5 billion in revenue, growing at least 35% year-over-year and representing 25% of total revenue.

  • The CEO underscored Flex's global operational scale, reporting more than 49 million square feet globally, including recent expansion in North America, and investments in AI-enabled systems and advanced automation. She stated, "We have led the shift towards regionalization and the impact is clear. Americas revenue for us rose to 49% in fiscal year '25, up from 38% in fiscal year '20, while Asia declined to 30%, down from 41%."

  • CFO Kevin S. Krumm reported, "First quarter revenue came in at $6.6 billion, up 4%, driven by strong data center growth across both cloud and power end markets." He added, "Gross profit totaled $596 million, and gross margin improved to 9.1%, up 130 basis points." Krumm also pointed out the acquisition of a new manufacturing site in Poland, which "doubles our power capacity in Europe, allowing us to meet the rising global demand for reliable data center power."

Outlook

  • Management updated FY26 expectations to revenue between $25.9 billion and $27.1 billion, with an increased midpoint by approximately $600 million. Adjusted operating margin is forecasted between 6% and 6.1%, and adjusted EPS between $2.86 and $3.06 per share.

  • The full-year data center business is expected to deliver approximately $6.5 billion in revenue, growing at least 35% year-over-year. Reliability Solutions segment outlook improved, now expected to be down low single digit to up mid-single digit. Agility Solutions is anticipated to grow in the low to mid-single-digit range.

  • For Q2, Flex projects revenue between $6.5 billion and $6.8 billion, adjusted operating income between $375 million and $415 million, and adjusted EPS between $0.70 and $0.78 per share.

Financial Results

  • Flex reported Q1 2026 revenue of $6.6 billion and adjusted EPS of $0.72. Gross margin increased to 9.1%.

  • Reliability Solutions revenue was $2.9 billion, down 2% year-over-year, while operating income improved to $172 million and segment margin expanded to 6%.

  • Agility Solutions revenue was $3.7 billion, up 10% year-over-year, with operating income of $240 million and margin of 6.5%.

  • Free cash flow for the quarter was $268 million. Net inventory was up 3% sequentially but down 11% year-over-year. Net CapEx totaled $131 million, and Flex repurchased $247 million of stock.

Q&A

  • Samik Chatterjee, JPMorgan: Asked why margin outlook is not improving with higher revenue guidance. CFO Krumm responded, "We held our prior margin guided range of 6% to 6.1%...We have brought in tariffs...that's largely low-calorie revenue and actually is a headwind to our margin performance."

  • Chatterjee: Inquired about data center trends and growth split between cloud and power. CEO Advaithi stated, "We feel very good about our 35% growth forecast...we're still in line with what we had said earlier that power will be stronger."

  • Mark Delaney, Goldman Sachs: Asked about hyperscalers doing more products in-house. Advaithi said, "Amazon's announcement really validates the fact that we needed the capability both for manufacturing capability, but also technology capability." On full-year guidance, Krumm explained, "You don't see that pass all the way through to EPS because we lost a little guide to guide in the interest and other line item."

  • Steven Fox, Fox Advisors: Asked about capacity constraints. Advaithi responded, "It's a good problem to have, where we have so much growth...My hope is that we continue to make investments in growth for AI infrastructure, both in power and cloud."

  • Ruplu Bhattacharya, BofA: Queried about tariff impacts. Krumm clarified, "Tariffs are going to have -- will not have a material impact on our growth rate...They do have an impact on margin performance."

  • Robert Stephen Barger, KeyBanc: Asked about customer engagement across the suite of IT integration and power products. Advaithi replied, "Most of them particularly the hyperscalers will be buying a whole suite of products...our ability to convert the compute and power coming together is becoming more and more reality."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions about margin leverage, capacity constraints, and the potential impact of tariffs, reflecting a cautiously optimistic but probing tone.

  • Management maintained a confident stance in prepared remarks and during Q&A, frequently reiterating strong performance and future growth. Advaithi stated, "I remain deeply confident in our strategy and the unique value we deliver."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment remains cautiously constructive, while management's tone remains consistently confident but slightly more explicit in addressing tariff-related headwinds.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue guidance midpoint increased by $600 million, and full-year data center revenue target is now $6.5 billion at 35% growth, a more explicit forecast than the prior quarter.

  • Tariff impacts are now directly incorporated into revenue guidance, whereas previously they were excluded due to uncertainty.

  • Segment outlook for Reliability and Agility Solutions is modestly improved compared to the previous quarter, reflecting stronger expectations for data center and networking.

  • Management maintains a conservative approach, but with increased clarity and confidence in addressing headwinds and investments.

  • Analysts continue to focus on margin sustainability, capacity, and exposure to tariffs, similar to last quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited macroeconomic uncertainty and the evolving tariff environment as ongoing risks.

  • CFO Krumm noted, "Tariffs are largely a pass-through for us. So you see that in this updated guide as well." He added that tariffs are a margin headwind but not material to revenue growth rates.

  • Investments in new capacity and technology are essential to meet rapidly growing data center demand, which could expose Flex to risks if demand slows or execution stumbles.

Final Takeaway

Flex delivered a record Q1 and raised its fiscal 2026 outlook, driven by robust data center growth and strategic expansion in power capacity, particularly in Europe. The company is confident in achieving its raised guidance, with data center revenue targeted at $6.5 billion for the year and strong margin performance despite headwinds from tariffs and ongoing investments. Management remains focused on high-growth end markets and continues to prioritize operational efficiency and capital allocation to support both organic and acquisitive growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Comcast releases names of Versant's board members
data.description:
Comcast (NASDAQ:CMCSA) announced the names of directors who will join the board of Versant Media Group, the networks-focused company spun out of the media and cable giant.

Led by chairman David Novak, longtime Comcast board member and former CEO of Yum! Brands, the new company's board will include:

  • Versant CEO Mark Lazarus
  • Rebecca Campbell, former chairman of International Content and Operations at Disney
  • Creighton Condon, counsel at law firm A&O Shearman
  • Michael Conway, former CEO of Starbucks North America
  • David Eun, founding advisor to Kanza AI
  • Gerald Hassell, former chairman and CEO of The Bank of New York Mellon Corporation
  • Scott Mahoney, chairman and CEO of Peter Millar LLC
  • Maritza Montiel, former Deputy CEO and VC of Deloitte & Touche LLP’s U.S. business
  • Len Potter, founder of Wildcat Capital Management.

Versant's board will consist of 10 members in total. The new company is expected to be spun off from Comcast by the end of 2025.

data.title: Sagimet gains as Canaccord Genuity starts with Buy on potential against MASH
data.description:
Sagimet Biosciences (NASDAQ:SGMT) traded higher on Thursday after Canaccord Genuity launched its coverage with a Buy recommendation, citing the potential of its lead asset denifanstat, targeted at a liver disease called metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH).

With a 12-month price target of $28, Canaccord analyst Edward Nash argued that the California-based biotech stands out from its peers in the MASH space, given its combination approach against the fatty liver disease.

“Sagimet is one of the more compelling names in the MASH space given their lead drug denifanstat’s differentiated mechanism of action,” Nash wrote, adding that the combo tying SGMT’s FASN inhibitor with the THR-ß agonist resmetirom could reach the market in 2034, leading to $1.7B in sales in 2035.

Sagimet (NASDAQ:SGMT), the only company with an FASN inhibitor in clinical development for MASH, has also launched a Phase I trial for TVB-3567, its second FASN inhibitor for which its Chinese partner, Ascletis, has posted positive Phase 3 data for acne.

While TVB-3567 is set to enter mid-stage development in 2026, and Nash hasn’t included TVB-3567 in his model, he argued that with ~50M Americans affected by acne and 5M seeking treatment, the skin condition “offers blockbuster potential for an effective oral treatment.”

data.title: Fannie Mae revises down 2025, 2026 mortgage rates, home price growth outlooks
data.description:
Fannie Mae (OTCQB:FNMA) revised its 2025 and 2026 mortgage rates and home price growth estimates lower, as part of the July 2025 Economic and Housing Outlook report.

Mortgage rates are now estimated to end 2025 and 2026 at 6.4% and 6.0%, respectively, compared to the last month's forecast of 6.5% and 6.1%.

Meanwhile, the 2025 and 2026 annual home price growth forecasts are now revised to 2.8% and 1.1%, respectively, compared to last month's 4.1% and 2.0%. The estimates are on a Q4/Q4 basis.

Home sales are now forecast at 4.85M units in 2025 and 5.35M units in 2026. The estimates are higher than the previous projections of 4.82M in 2025 and 5.25M in 2026.

Mortgage originations are now projected at $1.92T and $2.34T, respectively, for 2025 and 2026. The previous forecasts were $1.90T and $2.28T.

The financing solutions provider for residential mortgages has similarly revised its real GDP growth outlook for 2025 and 2026 to 1.3% and 2.3%, respectively, on a Q4/Q4 basis. This compares to the prior forecast of 1.4% and 2.2%.

data.title: Keurig Dr Pepper aims for double-digit energy market share as U.S. refreshment beverages surge 11%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Keurig Dr Pepper Inc. (KDP) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Timothy P. Cofer reported “strong second quarter results, closing out a very good first half of the year,” attributing performance to an “advantaged business model, execution and agility while operating in a dynamic environment.” He confirmed the company remains “on track to achieve our full year outlook, thanks to strong first half delivery and well calibrated back half plans.”
  • Cofer highlighted product innovation, stating, “Dr Pepper Blackberry ranks as the #1 new product in the category this year,” and noted momentum for 7UP and electrolyte, with the latter “the fastest-growing scaled brand in the category, benefiting from strong velocities, DSD enabled distribution expansion and product and packaging innovation.”
  • The acquisition of Dyla brands was announced, giving KDP “100% ownership of Dyla brands, a key player in powdered drink mixes and liquid water enhancers.” Cofer described this as a move to “expand our presence in an attractive and growing category.”
  • In energy, Cofer stated, “Our 4 complementary brands GHOST, C4, Bloom and Black Rifle now combine to represent over $1 billion in annual run rate net sales for KDP and are scaling rapidly.” He further explained, “With 7% market share already, KDP's energy portfolio is making quick progress against this target. And for comparison, just a few years ago, our share was below 1%.”
  • CFO Sudhanshu Shekhar Priyadarshi said, “Second quarter net sales increased 7.2% in constant currency. Our top line momentum was broad-based with double-digit gains in U.S. refreshment beverages, solid mid-single-digit growth in international and encouraging sequential improvement in U.S. coffee.”
  • Priyadarshi added, “Gross margin contracted 110 basis points versus the prior year due to inflationary pressures that more than offset pricing and productivity savings. Strong SG&A leverage served as a counterbalance.”

Outlook

  • Priyadarshi stated, “Our constant currency outlook is unchanged. We continue to expect mid-single-digit net sales growth with a bias towards the high end of the range and high single-digit earnings per share growth.”
  • Management projects “FX will represent approximately 0.5 percentage point headwind to the top and bottom line for the full year which equates to about a $0.01 impact to EPS.”
  • Guidance assumes sustained top line momentum but recognizes “cost pressures mount. As a result, we continue to expect some margin pressure in the back half which should contribute to a moderating EPS growth rate relative to the first half.”

Financial Results

  • Net sales grew 7% for the enterprise, driven by both price and volume mix, with U.S. refreshment beverages up “almost 11% in the quarter.”
  • Volume mix in U.S. refreshment beverages increased 9.5%, including a 6.6 percentage point contribution from GHOST. Net price realization added 1% to the top line.
  • In U.S. coffee, net sales declined 0.2% but showed improvement from the prior quarter. Net price realization strengthened to 3.6%, with volume mix down 3.8%.
  • Free cash flow for the quarter was $325 million. Leverage stood at 3.3x, with a stated long-term goal of 2.5x or lower.
  • Gross margin contracted 110 basis points year-over-year, primarily due to inflation, but operating income grew 7% as margins held steady.

Q&A

  • Christopher Michael Carey, Wells Fargo: Asked about the split between partner assets and core in U.S. refreshment beverages and the runway for Dr Pepper. CEO Cofer responded, “We expect USRB segment growth will remain robust. And we expect, as you saw in the first half, it will be a balance of both solid base business growth as well as new partner growth and importantly, contribution from GHOST and the energy portfolio.”
  • Peter K. Grom, UBS: Sought clarity on coffee trends and expectations for the second half. Cofer answered, “While I'm encouraged by the progress in U.S. coffee in the second quarter, there's no doubt as we roll into the back half, the segment will face some challenges... Commodity inflation will build... tariff impacts will become prominent.”
  • Bonnie Lee Herzog, Goldman Sachs: Asked about sustainability of top-line growth and margin outlook. Priyadarshi replied, “We are also focused on SG&A and overhead. And all of those things will help us deliver -- continue to deliver MSD sales and HSD EPS.” He acknowledged, “in Q2, gross margin contracted, but we were expecting it... second half, we expect some operating margin pressure, but we will still grow profit dollar.”
  • Kaumil S. Gajrawala, Jefferies: Inquired about DSD network infrastructure for Dr Pepper expansion. Cofer explained, “Distribution transitions like this do come generally with some short-term disruption and clearly, some initial investment, but we're prepared for all of that... And we're confident long term, you'll see us continue to unlock substantial commercial and financial outcomes as it relates DSD expansion.”
  • Robert Edward Ottenstein, Evercore: Asked about pricing dynamics and consumer behavior. Priyadarshi noted, “Specific to net price, we have seen positive contribution year-to-date primarily been driven by CSD. We also announced a typical CSD price increase that took impact in Q1, which should continue to flow through to our results for the entire year.” Cofer added, “We're seeing a fairly resilient consumer even in this backdrop of an inflationary and somewhat uncertain environment.”
  • Dara Warren Mohsenian, Morgan Stanley: Questioned marketing strategy and digital transformation. Cofer stated, “We are putting data, technology and digital at the center of the marketing flywheel... A place to start seeing some of that show up will be brand Dr. Pepper.”
  • Filippo Falorni, Citi: Asked about the protein beverage space and Dyla acquisition. Cofer explained, “We will continue to evaluate those places which are more white spaces. And I believe protein is one of those. No doubt consumers are looking for more functional attributes and the beverages and protein is part of that trend.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was cautiously optimistic, seeking clarity on growth sustainability, margin outlook, and the impact of new initiatives, yet pressing on challenges in coffee and margin pressures.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing strategic execution and resilience. Cofer used language such as “we are confident,” “we feel good,” and “we are prepared” in response to questions about growth and operational challenges.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained disciplined, with slightly increased caution regarding cost pressures and coffee segment headwinds. Analysts continued to probe on margin and sustainability, reflecting a consistent but slightly more probing sentiment quarter-over-quarter.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for mid-single-digit net sales growth and high single-digit EPS growth is reaffirmed, but management now anticipates FX to be a smaller headwind (0.5 percentage point versus 1 percentage point previously).
  • U.S. refreshment beverages continued to deliver double-digit growth, with GHOST and partner brands playing a larger role in segment results compared to Q1.
  • U.S. coffee showed sequential improvement versus Q1's decline, though management reiterated expectations for subdued segment performance due to commodity inflation and tariffs.
  • New product and portfolio innovation, as well as the Dyla acquisition, were highlighted as incremental growth drivers in Q2, indicating a shift toward expansion in functional and wellness categories.
  • Management confidence in cash flow generation improved, with free cash flow and leverage metrics showing sequential strengthening.
  • Analysts’ line of questioning remained focused on sustainability, cost pressures, and the impact of strategic initiatives, with more attention paid to how new acquisitions and product launches will drive future growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Rising cost pressures, particularly from tariffs and commodity inflation, are cited as ongoing challenges.
  • Management acknowledged the need to monitor consumer elasticity in response to additional pricing, especially in the coffee segment.
  • Supply chain transitions, such as DSD expansion for Dr Pepper, could result in short-term disruption and initial investment requirements.
  • Uncertainty remains around future trade policy and its impact on input costs and margins.
  • Analysts raised concerns about margin sustainability, elasticity in coffee, and the ability to offset inflation and tariff effects.

Final Takeaway

Keurig Dr Pepper delivered robust second quarter results highlighted by double-digit growth in U.S. refreshment beverages, expanded market share in energy, and the successful integration of new product innovations and acquisitions. Despite mounting cost pressures, particularly in coffee and from tariffs, management reaffirmed its full-year guidance and signaled confidence in both short-term execution and long-term strategic priorities. The company’s focus on brand innovation, route-to-market expansion, and disciplined cost management is expected to support continued growth and value creation for shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Deutsche Bank stock gains as Q2 earnings benefit from strong trading income
data.description:
Deutsche Bank (NYSE:DB) stock climbed 8.5% in Frankfurt trading and touched the highest level in more than 10 years on Thursday after the bank's trading results surpassed Wall Street expectations.

Furthermore, the bank remains on track to meet its full-year 2025 targets, and "we remain comfortable with our capital position and reiterate our commitment to outperforming our 8-billion-euro distribution target," Chief Financial Officer James Von Moltke said.

Excluding Postbank litigation costs, Q2 profit before tax was €2.4B, up 34% Y/Y, the company said.

Q2 income from trading bonds and currencies climbed 11% Y/Y to €2.28B, beating the €2.20B consensus estimate, according to Bloomberg.

Q2 pre-provision profit of €2.84 declined from €3.31B in Q1 and jumped from €887M in last year's Q2.

Q2 net interest income of €3.84B climbed from €3.67B in the previous quarter and €3.02B in the year-ago period.

Provision for credit losses of €423M dropped from €471M in Q1 and €476M in last year's Q2.

Q2 adjusted costs fell 1% Y/Y to €5.0B, in line with guidance.

Common equity tier 1 capital ratio of 14.2% improved from 13.8% in Q1 and 13.5% in Q2 2024.

Earlier, Deutsche Bank GAAP EPS of €0.48 misses by €0.10, revenue of €7.8B beats by €70M

data.title: Dow outlines $800M Q3 EBITDA target amid dividend cut and $6B cash support actions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Dow Inc. (DOW) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO James R. Fitterling opened by addressing the "prolonged down cycle our industry has been experiencing," noting it was "further amplified this quarter by heightened trade and geopolitical uncertainties, which have strained profitability across our industry." He reported Dow's net sales of $10.1 billion for the quarter, down 7% year-over-year and 3% sequentially, with declines across all operating segments. Fitterling announced a "50% dividend reduction effective in the third quarter of this year," stating, "this is the most prudent way to maintain financial flexibility and maximize long-term value for our shareholders." He emphasized commitment to a "competitive dividend across the economic cycle" and detailed near-term cash measures, including $2.4 billion received from the Diamond Infrastructure Solutions partnership and expectations for $250 million from two noncore divestitures. He also highlighted the shutdown of three upstream assets in Europe and an accelerated cost savings target, now aiming for $400 million in 2025.
  • COO Karen S. Carter reported on segment performance, noting polyethylene prices in North America fell $0.03 per pound in April but recovered in June. She confirmed, "following 3 consecutive months of industry inventory decreases, we see a favorable backdrop supporting further price increases." She cited the start-up of the Poly-7 polyethylene train and volume growth in the U.S. and Canada. Carter described challenging conditions in Industrial Intermediates & Infrastructure, but noted higher volumes in industrial solutions and the completion of a new alkoxylation unit in Texas to support future growth.
  • CFO Jeffrey L. Tate stated, "we anticipate our third quarter EBITDA to be approximately $800 million, a $100 million improvement from the second quarter." Tate discussed $400 million in targeted cost savings for this year and emphasized $6 billion in near-term cash support from asset sales, cost reductions, and the NOVA litigation resolution.

Outlook

  • Dow projects third quarter EBITDA of approximately $800 million, citing expectations for "a sequential improvement in polyethylene integrated margins as well as higher volumes from our growth investments." The company anticipates cost reduction savings of $400 million for the year. Management acknowledged continued uncertainty in "macroeconomic backdrop," but expects improvements in Packaging & Specialty Plastics and Industrial Intermediates & Infrastructure, with a slight sequential EBITDA decline in Performance Materials & Coatings due to seasonal and market factors. Management stated, "should we become aware of significant changes during the quarter, we will share timely updates as appropriate."

Financial Results

  • Net sales for Q2 2025 were $10.1 billion, down 7% from the prior year and 3% sequentially, with declines in all segments. EBITDA was reported at $703 million. The Diamond Infrastructure Solutions partnership generated $2.4 billion in cash, and two noncore product line divestitures are expected to bring in $250 million. Dow is targeting $1 billion in annual cost savings by 2026, with $400 million expected this year. The company executed a debt-neutral $1 billion bond issue and expects to receive $1.2 billion from the NOVA litigation resolution, with more than $6 billion in near-term cash support projected from these actions.

Q&A

  • Vincent Stephen Andrews, Morgan Stanley, questioned the rationale for maintaining a fixed dividend. CFO Tate responded that "a lot of analysis and forward-looking modeling" led to the dividend reduction and that "mid-cycle earnings of the company has [not] changed. I think the timeline is what's changed."
  • Hassan Ijaz Ahmed, Alembic Global Advisors, inquired about the dividend philosophy and polyethylene supply-demand. CEO Fitterling indicated the dividend is "an important part of our stock ownership" and emphasized maintaining flexibility while focusing on earnings growth. On polyethylene, Fitterling said closures were mainly in Europe and "polyethylene continues to grow above GDP rates."
  • Michael Joseph Sison, Wells Fargo, asked about the path to higher adjusted EBIT in light of improved operating rates and margins. Carter explained that April's export disruptions led to a $0.03 per pound price drop, but June saw recovery and momentum for price increases in July. She cited the Poly-7 train as fully sold out and key to higher-value segments.
  • Jeffrey John Zekauskas, JPMorgan, discussed the Path2Zero project and working capital. Fitterling said the project is delayed until earnings growth returns, and Tate expects working capital to improve in the second half of the year.
  • David L. Begleiter, Deutsche Bank, asked about mid-cycle EBITDA and anticompetitive behavior. Fitterling referenced an average EBITDA of $8.6 billion for 2018-2021 and described ongoing regulatory and trade actions to address pricing pressures.
  • Other analysts focused on use of cash from the dividend reduction, duration of overcapacity, and regional market dynamics, with management emphasizing flexibility, balance sheet strength, and ongoing cost discipline.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns about the dividend cut, supply-demand imbalance, and earnings growth timeline, often pressing for clarity on strategic decisions and recovery expectations. The tone was neutral to slightly negative, with persistent questioning on cash priorities and market headwinds.
  • Management maintained a measured and rational tone in prepared remarks but shifted to more defensive and explanatory language during Q&A, frequently emphasizing flexibility, long-term strategy, and operational discipline. The tone was less confident compared to the previous quarter, with increased focus on near-term risk mitigation and uncertainty.
  • Compared to Q1, both analysts and management displayed heightened caution and a more urgent focus on downside protection, especially regarding the dividend and macroeconomic challenges.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The most significant change from Q1 is the 50% dividend reduction, which was not anticipated previously and marks a new approach to capital allocation in light of persistent earnings pressure. In Q1, Dow delayed the Path2Zero project and signaled $1 billion in 2025 CapEx cuts; these actions are now joined by further asset divestitures and a sharpened focus on cash preservation.
  • Q2 guidance highlighted an $800 million Q3 EBITDA target, $100 million above Q2, whereas Q1 guidance was for flat sequential EBITDA. Polyethylene margin recovery and volume growth are more prominent themes in Q2, while Q1 was more focused on cost-cutting ramp-up and managing macro volatility.
  • Management’s tone in Q2 is more guarded, frequently referencing uncertainty and the need for flexibility, compared to a more proactive stance in Q1.
  • Analysts in Q2 were more focused on dividend sustainability, duration of the downturn, and the details of supply-demand adjustment, reflecting increased market anxiety.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "heightened trade and geopolitical uncertainties," oversupply from new market entrants, and "anticompetitive economics" as key risks. The company is engaged in regulatory action and asset shutdowns, particularly in Europe, to address these challenges.
  • Uncertainty around tariffs, export disruptions, and volatile global demand continues to weigh on visibility for earnings recovery.
  • Analysts expressed concern about the need for further portfolio restructuring, JV performance (particularly Sadara), and the timing for resumption of large-scale growth projects.

Final Takeaway

Dow’s management has responded to a prolonged industry downturn by cutting the dividend by 50%, accelerating cost reductions, securing over $6 billion in near-term cash support, and executing key asset divestitures. The company projects a sequential EBITDA improvement to $800 million in Q3, banking on polyethylene margin expansion and volume growth from new assets. While maintaining a disciplined focus on flexibility and operational execution, Dow is actively managing risks from global trade, oversupply, and uncertain demand, aiming to position itself for profitable growth as market conditions eventually recover.
